metadata.summary: Tailwind CSS utility-first framework configured via tailwind.config.ts with explicit content globs, token-driven theme, and canonical class order via prettier-plugin-tailwindcss.

content.title: Tailwind CSS
content.summary: Tailwind CSS is a utility-first framework configured via tailwind.config.ts (v3) or a CSS-first @theme block (v4). Configure content globs explicitly to include all templates; theme tokens (colors, spacing, radii) live in the config or @theme, never as hardcoded hex in components. Use prettier-plugin-tailwindcss to canonicalize class order. @apply is allowed only in globals.css for thin reset helpers (≤3 utilities) — abstractions belong in components.

why.p: Utility classes keep styling co-located with markup, eliminate dead CSS, and give agents a stable, greppable styling surface. The JIT compiler ships only used classes. Canonical class order via Prettier makes agent-generated diffs readable and conflict-free. Token-driven config enforces design consistency without a separate CSS variable layer.

list.item: Greenfield app or rewrite where you control HTML and want utility-first styling.
Multi-developer or multi-agent project needing a stable, greppable styling surface.
Fast iteration on visual design with token control via config.
Pairing with React, Vue, Svelte, or HTMX where templating lives near markup.
Brownfield app with mature CSS-in-JS or BEM conventions — mixing causes specificity wars.
Email templates, PDFs, or print-first surfaces (Tailwind's reset and JIT do not target these).
Library/SDK shipping CSS — utility classes leak global resets onto consumer apps.
Strict design system that bans "magic numbers" — utilities make ad-hoc spacing too easy.

rule.statement: Configure the `content` array explicitly in tailwind.config.ts (v3) or @source directives (v4). Include all template sources: src/, components/, MDX, and server-side templates. Never rely on autodetection.
rule.rationale: JIT only scans paths inside content globs. Classes outside the glob are purged silently — the component renders without styles and there is no build error.

rule.statement: Write base styles for mobile with no prefix, then use `sm:`, `md:`, `lg:`, `xl:` to scale up. Never write desktop-only styles and use max-* breakpoints to override down.
rule.rationale: Mobile-first produces additive, conflict-free breakpoint chains. Overriding down with max-* creates specificity tangles and requires more CSS classes to maintain.

rule.statement: All color values must come from design tokens defined in tailwind.config.ts `theme.extend.colors` (v3) or `@theme` block (v4). Never write arbitrary hex values like `bg-[#2563eb]` in component files.
rule.rationale: Hardcoded hex in components defeats centralized token management. When brand colors change, every component file needs a grep-and-replace instead of a single config edit.

rule.statement: Limit `@apply` to thin reset helpers in globals.css with at most 3 utilities per rule. Abstractions that need more than 3 utilities belong in React/Vue components, not in CSS.
rule.rationale: Long @apply chains recreate the maintenance problem that component extraction solves. Agents fed "@apply is fine" produce sprawling stylesheet abstractions instead of composable components.

rule.statement: For v3: use tailwind.config.ts with `theme.extend` and `plugins: [require(...)]`. For v4: use a CSS-first `@theme` block in globals.css; do not write a tailwind.config.ts. Never mix APIs.
rule.rationale: Training data mixes v3 and v4 examples. A v3-style config.ts is silently ignored by v4; a @theme block in CSS is not processed by v3. Specifying the version prevents silent misconfiguration.

rule.statement: Install `prettier-plugin-tailwindcss` and configure it in `.prettierrc`. Enforce class order in CI with `prettier --check`. Never commit hand-sorted class strings.
rule.rationale: Different agents produce different class orders. Canonical order makes diffs readable and prevents spurious merge conflicts in class attribute lines.

rule.statement: Add every Tailwind plugin to the `plugins` array in tailwind.config.ts (v3) or import it with `@plugin` (v4) before using its utility classes. Never assume plugins are auto-registered.
rule.rationale: Plugins like @tailwindcss/forms are not bundled — they must be explicitly registered. Missing registration produces no build error; the classes are silently absent at runtime.

example.title: tailwind.config.ts (v3)
v4 CSS-First Config
Common Layout Patterns

section.name: dark-mode-strategy
section.note: Use `darkMode: 'class'` (v3) or `.dark` selector (v4) for explicit control. Use `media` only when you do not need a manual toggle. Never switch strategies after launch — it changes all component dark: selectors.

example.title: ThemeProvider Implementation

antipattern.description: Agents mix v3 tailwind.config.ts and v4 @theme block in the same project.
antipattern.reason: v4 ignores tailwind.config.ts; v3 ignores @theme. Specify the major version in every agent prompt. Check tailwindcss package version before generating config.

antipattern.description: Agents install @tailwindcss/forms but forget `strategy: 'class'`, breaking existing inputs.
antipattern.reason: Without `strategy: 'class'`, the forms plugin applies global input resets that override component styles. The strategy must match the project's existing form styling approach.

antipattern.description: Agents write `@apply` chains with 8–15 utilities in a global stylesheet.
antipattern.reason: Long @apply chains recreate the BEM stylesheet maintenance problem. Anything needing more than 3 utilities is a component, not a utility alias.

antipattern.description: After adding a new template file (Blade, Django, ERB), agents forget to update the content glob.
antipattern.reason: JIT only scans files matching content glob patterns. New template types silently produce unstyled output until the config is updated.

template.purpose: v3 Tailwind config with extended colors, fontFamily, spacing, and plugins.
v4 CSS-first config with @theme block for brand tokens.
CI guard: Prettier + ESLint class-order check.
